paragraphs.body: Son of Mr. Green Jeans Model Essay Step one: Prep your starter. My life is mostly preparation, always having a plan. Something to follow. It is honestly stressful. It’s the constant feeling that something is going to go wrong and that what I am doing isn’t enough. Even so I keep on planning, or I will go insane. A goal is what I need to thrive, and I can’t really remember when it wasn’t needed. Just when not having one was my downfall.

Step two: Mix the dough. All my feelings in a ball of dough. It’s messy, it’s sticky but that is okay. There is time to go round out. Middle school is a huge milestone and change. For me it was everything. I went to a above average student to just average. Everything was competitive and it wasn’t even the school I wanted to go to. I only went because my mom said, “somethings should just be hobbies” and that was okay. I understood what she meant but it still hurt. It’s the money people tend to think that you should strive for money over passion. That can make you miserable. I was miserable.

annotations.text: average. Everything was competitive and it wasn’t even the school I wanted to go to. I only went because my mom said, “somethings should just be hobbies” and that was okay.
annotations.comment: This is a compelling passage, but its moments like this that would benefit from some more concrete and specific details.

paragraphs.body: Step three: Take a nap. Just like me dough must nap for 30 minutes to 4 hours. The longer the better I feel. It’s hard for me to get up to go to school. I had no one to look forward to. The classes were boring to me, and I was lonely. I remember the smell of books at lunch time instead of food. I didn’t eat since I had no one to sit with and the food wasn’t that appetizing. I would nap for thirty minutes instead.

annotations.text: lonely. I remember the smell of books at lunch time instead of food. I didn’t eat since I had no one to sit with and the food wasn’t that appetizing. I would nap for thirty minutes instead.
annotations.comment: Here is a moment when your writing is at its clearest, its strongest.
annotations.context_left: ng to me, and I was

paragraphs.body: Step four: Give it a tuck. Getting tucked in as a child to bed is always a comforting experience. I miss that comfort. I do not like hugs. They are healthy for humans, but they just make me uncomfortable. I want to feel comfort, but I don’t want to feel trapped or forced. Maybe I just sensed that their motives weren’t very pure of heart. I was a very lonely child. Everyone deserves a hug that feels right.

Step five: Shape the dough. Young humans are like dough, you must shape them so they can be the best they can be. So why don’t we care about mental health? I get really sick at being told how I should feel. Being told how to feel better is one thing but it working is another. People may tell them giving advice is helping but it typically doesn’t. I just wanted someone to listen without questioning me or giving advice. I want someone to care about how I feel.
Step six: Let it rise. When they tell you to be the bigger person. That’s annoying.

Step seven: Keep rising. Sometimes I feel like I’m deflating more than rising. It makes me feel bad. I tended to have my highs and lows. I would be extremely happy for weeks and then I would crash hard. It was like I bottled up all my emotions and then shook the bottle. It wasn’t just sadness it turned into anger and jealousy. Eventually it just became numb. It wasn’t that I didn’t feel anything. It was that I felt so much it just all felt the same. There is only so much pain you can feel.

Step eight: Bake it. Finally, the baking. We are constantly baking our personalities and likes, and little actions can change them entirely. Taking your loaf out too early or late. Maybe even forgetting to turn on the oven. You can get hurt. It sucks. Sometimes you just need to have someone to help you out. Not everyone has that, and I haven’t had it for a long time. If you only have yourself that is ok, take your time. Step nine: Let it cool. Just like anger if you don’t let it cool down all the way it goes bad. Mushy feelings, soggy crusts, rubbery but not quiet. I had to learn that the hard way to just walk away. People couldn’t help me with my emotions, mostly they made me up when they asked me to talk about it. Sometimes I don’t know what to do. Step ten: Enjoy yourself.

annotations.comment: Hello, The central focusing idea of this writing is a creative reflection on some of your formative life experiences. Honestly, the form of this piece is really great, one of the best strengths of the piece. I believe that even if a reader is not a baker, per se, they will have a clear understanding of this process, and it’s lovely to follow along with these baking instructions while getting to know you through your experiences. The word that stands out the most to me in this piece is dough. That might be the most obvious answer, but it is clear that the narrator has a feeling of development, of growing and, in a sense, being shaped by their environment. Even though this development is the result of you facing many challenges, the form of a recipe, and the metaphor of dough, bring me a sense of joy and wonder—it’s an extremely interesting perspective on growing up. I can easily see that the primary audience of this piece could be the parents of adolescent children. The purpose of this writing is to inspire a deeper level of care in raising one’s offspring. If you want to make bread, you just can’t put water and flour in a bowl and throw it in the over, and, in similar fashion, how you raise children shapes their experiences and perspectives for life. What I’ve learned about you through reading this piece is that you have a keen eye for using metaphor to deepen your understanding of the world. The metaphor of baking and the accompanying terms and instructions are clear and focused throughout the writing—you don’t veer off into tangential metaphors or lose sight of the subject at hand. It’s a real please to read. Thank You for Sharing,
